content.text: Multi-head attention (MHA) is the core relational operator inside every transformer layer. Rather than running a single attention computation over the full model dimension
independent scaled dot-product attention operations — called
— in parallel. Each head learns a distinct projection of queries, keys, and values, allowing the model to simultaneously attend to multiple types of relationships across the sequence.

LLMs used in agentic systems must process long, richly structured prompts: system instructions, tool schemas, conversation history, retrieved documents, and partially completed plans — often in a single context window. A single attention head can only represent one relational pattern per layer. Multi-head attention gives the model the capacity to track
Empirically, different heads specialize. In GPT-2, Elhage et al. (2021) showed that some heads are

that copy patterns from earlier in the sequence, while others resolve coreference or track positional distance. This specialization emerges from gradient descent, not explicit design. For agents, the practical consequence is that the model can simultaneously maintain awareness of the current subtask goal, the tool it should call next, and the conversation turn that established the constraint — with no explicit routing logic.

Each head attends to the input in its own subspace. One head might resolve pronoun references; another might track verb-argument structure. The diversity emerges from training without explicit supervision.
All heads compute simultaneously via batched matrix multiplication, so MHA adds only a constant factor over single-head attention in wall-clock time.
, making MHA a drop-in layer without shape changes elsewhere in the transformer.
By projecting query, key, and value independently per head, MHA effectively learns
subspace projections that together cover the information the model needs — more expressive than a single

Grouped-query attention (GQA) and multi-query attention (MQA) are straightforward reductions of MHA that share K/V projections across head groups, reducing KV cache memory at inference time without rewriting the architecture.
The attention score matrix has shape
tokens this becomes prohibitive without FlashAttention or sparse attention.
Adding heads past the point of gradient signal saturation hurts rather than helps. GPT-2 Small (d_model=768) uses 12 heads; adding a 13th would make

During autoregressive decoding, every generated token appends a K/V pair across all heads and layers. For 32 layers and 32 heads at bfloat16, a 100K-token context can require tens of gigabytes of KV cache.
Studies of BERT and GPT-style models consistently show that pruning 30–50% of heads incurs negligible perplexity loss, suggesting many heads are redundant after training.

Different tasks require different masks (causal, padding, cross-attention). Incorrect mask shapes are a common source of training bugs that are difficult to detect without explicit attention weight inspection.

attrs.alt: Multi-Head Attention Architecture: Input embeddings flow through h parallel attention heads (each applying scaled dot-product attention in a lower-dimensional subspace), then outputs are concatenated and projected back to d_model.

content.text: The diagram shows the canonical MHA flow from "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017). Each head receives the full input
-dimensional subspace determined by its learned projections. The concatenation restores the full
blends head contributions into a single contextual representation per token.
MHA is the default choice whenever you are building on or studying transformer-based language models. You engage with it directly in three common agentic-AI scenarios:
When fine-tuning with LoRA or full fine-tuning, you adapt the W_Q, W_K, W_V, and W_O matrices. Understanding which heads handle which relation types guides LoRA rank selection.
Some agentic tasks require cross-attention between a query (e.g., a tool description) and a key-value corpus (e.g., retrieved documents). Implementing cross-MHA requires only changing the K/V source.

When serving LLMs at scale, the KV cache bottleneck is proportional to the number of K/V heads. Switching from MHA to GQA (as in LLaMA-2 and Mistral) requires directly reducing the number of K/V projections.
Plotting attention weight matrices across heads is a practical diagnostic for checking whether the model attends to the right tokens. Unexpected attention patterns can indicate prompt format bugs.
Avoid reimplementing MHA from scratch in production. Use
(PyTorch 2.0+) which dispatches to FlashAttention automatically when the input dtype and device allow it.

The implementation below shows the full MHA computation step by step. Understanding this code makes it possible to modify attention patterns, add custom masks, or adapt the mechanism for cross-attention.

import math
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F


def scaled_dot_product_attention(
    Q: torch.Tensor,
    K: torch.Tensor,
    V: torch.Tensor,
    mask: torch.Tensor | None = None,
) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    """
    Compute scaled dot-product attention.

    Args:
        Q: Query matrix  shape (..., seq_q, d_k)
        K: Key matrix    shape (..., seq_k, d_k)
        V: Value matrix  shape (..., seq_k, d_v)
        mask: Optional boolean mask (True = keep, False = mask out)

    Returns:
        output: shape (..., seq_q, d_v)
        weights: attention weights shape (..., seq_q, seq_k)
    """
    d_k = Q.size(-1)
    # Step 1: Dot products between every query and every key
    scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(d_k)  # (..., seq_q, seq_k)

    # Step 2: Apply causal or padding mask (set masked positions to -inf)
    if mask is not None:
        scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float("-inf"))

    # Step 3: Softmax to obtain attention weights
    weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)  # (..., seq_q, seq_k)

    # Step 4: Weighted sum of values
    output = torch.matmul(weights, V)   # (..., seq_q, d_v)
    return output, weights
The scaled dot-product function above is the building block. The full multi-head wrapper splits projections into head-sized chunks, applies the attention per head in parallel, then concatenates:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math


class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    """
    Multi-head attention from 'Attention Is All You Need'.
    Works for self-attention (Q=K=V=X) and cross-attention (K=V come from encoder).
    """

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, dropout: float = 0.0):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0, "d_model must be divisible by num_heads"
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads

        # Single fused projection for all heads is more efficient than h separate layers
        self.W_q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_o = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def split_heads(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Reshape (batch, seq, d_model) -> (batch, heads, seq, d_k)."""
        batch, seq, _ = x.shape
        x = x.view(batch, seq, self.num_heads, self.d_k)
        return x.transpose(1, 2)  # (batch, heads, seq, d_k)

    def forward(
        self,
        query: torch.Tensor,
        key: torch.Tensor,
        value: torch.Tensor,
        mask: torch.Tensor | None = None,
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        batch = query.size(0)

        # Step 1: Project and split into heads
        Q = self.split_heads(self.W_q(query))  # (B, H, T_q, d_k)
        K = self.split_heads(self.W_k(key))    # (B, H, T_k, d_k)
        V = self.split_heads(self.W_v(value))  # (B, H, T_k, d_k)

        # Step 2: Scaled dot-product attention per head (parallel)
        scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.d_k)
        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float("-inf"))
        weights = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        weights = self.dropout(weights)
        attended = torch.matmul(weights, V)     # (B, H, T_q, d_k)

        # Step 3: Concatenate heads and project
        attended = attended.transpose(1, 2).contiguous()  # (B, T_q, H, d_k)
        attended = attended.view(batch, -1, self.num_heads * self.d_k)  # (B, T_q, d_model)
        return self.W_o(attended)
The following examples demonstrate using both the custom implementation and PyTorch's built-in MHA, including the causal mask required for decoder-only models and the

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# ---- Using the custom implementation ----
d_model, num_heads, seq_len, batch = 512, 8, 64, 4
mha = MultiHeadAttention(d_model=d_model, num_heads=num_heads)

x = torch.randn(batch, seq_len, d_model)
# Self-attention: Q = K = V = x
out_self = mha(x, x, x)
print("Self-attention output:", out_self.shape)  # (4, 64, 512)

# Causal mask for autoregressive decoding
causal_mask = torch.tril(torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len)).unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0)
out_causal = mha(x, x, x, mask=causal_mask)
print("Causal attention output:", out_causal.shape)  # (4, 64, 512)

# ---- PyTorch built-in (production use) ----
torch_mha = nn.MultiheadAttention(
    embed_dim=d_model,
    num_heads=num_heads,
    dropout=0.1,
    batch_first=True,   # Expects (batch, seq, d_model) — set True!
)
out_torch, attn_weights = torch_mha(x, x, x, need_weights=False)
print("PyTorch MHA output:", out_torch.shape)         # (4, 64, 512)

# ---- Grouped-query attention (GQA) style — share K/V across head groups ----
# PyTorch 2.0+ supports this natively via F.scaled_dot_product_attention
# with key_padding_mask and is_causal arguments
out_sdpa = torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention(
    query=x.view(batch, num_heads, seq_len, d_model // num_heads),
    key=x.view(batch, num_heads, seq_len, d_model // num_heads),
    value=x.view(batch, num_heads, seq_len, d_model // num_heads),
    is_causal=True,        # Applies causal mask automatically — no allocations
)
print("SDPA (flash-backed) output:", out_sdpa.shape)  # (4, 8, 64, 64)
mask tensor — PyTorch generates it on the fly inside the kernel. This matters at long context lengths where the mask alone would exceed available memory.
GPT-4 and LLaMA-2 both use MHA as their relational core, but they apply it differently:

GPT-4 is estimated to use a decoder-only transformer with hundreds of layers. Each layer applies causal MHA — the mask is a lower-triangular matrix that prevents each token from attending to tokens it has not yet generated. The K/V cache stores one K matrix and one V matrix per head per layer per token. Serving a 100K-token context with 128 layers and 128 heads at bfloat16 requires roughly:
This cost is why grouped-query attention was introduced.
LLaMA-2 70B uses 64 query heads but only 8 K/V heads, grouped 8-to-1. This reduces the KV cache by
vs. standard MHA with no measurable quality degradation on most benchmarks. The HuggingFace
module implements this by repeating the K and V projections to match the query head count before computing attention — a simple

silently drops the head dimension unless explicitly unsqueezed.
In cross-attention (e.g., in an encoder-decoder or RAG retrieval step), K and V come from a different sequence than Q. Using
is a logic bug that produces plausible-looking but incorrect output.
is invalid. Always check divisibility. Most implementations raise an assertion, but some silently truncate.
In batched sequences with variable lengths, padding tokens should be masked from attending to and being attended to. Omitting the
causes the model to attend to padding, which is noise in the key-value sequence.
Materializing the full mask at long context.
float32 mask tensor requires 64 GB of memory. Use
or pass the mask as a boolean, which is 8x smaller.
PyTorch 2.0+ automatically selects FlashAttention, memory-efficient attention, or the math backend depending on hardware. No manual kernel selection needed.
to keep tensors in the intuitive
Validate this in the constructor with
. Choose head counts that are powers of two when possible.
Use bias=False for W_Q, W_K, W_V in decoder-only models.
Most modern architectures drop attention projection biases; they add parameters without significant quality improvement.
Log attention entropy during early training.

Low entropy (collapsed to a few tokens) indicates the softmax is saturating. High entropy (uniform distribution) indicates the model is not yet learning. Both are diagnostic of issues solvable by adjusting learning rate or initialisation.
Choose the right variant for your memory budget.
MHA for full quality in small-context tasks. GQA (8–32 KV heads) for production LLM serving. MQA (1 KV head) for extremely memory-constrained inference.
Attention has three cost dimensions: compute, memory, and KV cache.

activations. FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022) fuses the softmax and value-weighted sum into a single SRAM-resident tile loop, reducing peak memory to
while matching numerical output exactly. This is why
is strongly preferred over manual matrix multiplication for attention.
During autoregressive decoding, previously computed K and V tensors are cached and reused. Cache size for one model layer is

. Summed over all layers, this is the dominant memory consumer when serving 128K+ context windows. GQA and prefix sharing (reusing the KV cache prefix across requests with the same system prompt) are the two most impactful mitigations.
— PyTorch's standard implementation. Dispatches to optimized CUDA kernels. Use
(PyTorch 2.0+) — lower-level function that selects FlashAttention automatically. Preferred for custom architectures.
(Tri Dao, 2023) — CUDA kernels that deliver 2–4x speedup over standard attention at long sequences. Integrated into HuggingFace Transformers via
(Meta) — provides memory-efficient attention and custom sparse attention patterns as drop-in replacements. Supports
— all model implementations expose attention weights via
— HuggingFace's automatic sparsity for padding via
. Skips computation on padding tokens in non-causal settings.
— for custom attention patterns (e.g., sliding-window + global tokens as in Longformer), writing a custom Triton kernel is more practical than modifying PyTorch's C++ internals.
GQA offers the best trade-off for most production serving scenarios: it retains full query-head expressiveness while reducing KV cache memory by
is the number of KV head groups.

Because attention is soft and unbounded, a malicious input that places highly salient tokens early in the context can dominate attention scores across many heads. In retrieval-augmented generation, an injected document can direct the model's attention away from legitimate instruction tokens. Mitigation: place the system prompt
retrieved documents, or use instruction-hierarchy fine-tuning that trains the model to treat system-prompt tokens with higher attention priority.

In multi-tenant LLM serving, a shared KV cache prefix for a common system prompt can be timed to infer information about other users' prompts (through cache hit/miss latency). If the KV cache is not tenant-isolated, this is a side-channel leak. Mitigate with per-tenant prefix separation.

During training, attention weights can degenerate: all heads attending to the same positions (typically BOS/EOS tokens) indicates gradient-signal collapse. This produces a model that generates plausible-looking but uncontrolled output. Monitor attention entropy per layer; values near zero (peaked distributions) across all heads warrant training checkpoint inspection.

The softmax inside attention is numerically unstable if logits are large. The standard fix (shifting by the row maximum before exp) is implemented in all standard libraries, but custom implementations sometimes omit it. Always use
Think of multi-head attention as a panel of

specialists reading the same document simultaneously. Each specialist is looking for a different type of relationship — one tracks pronoun references, another watches for causal chains, a third monitors tool names and their arguments. They each write a short summary of what they found (the head output), and those summaries are stapled together and run through one final editor (W^O) who synthesizes them into a single coherent representation per word.
This is why MHA is more powerful than a single larger attention head. A single head of dimension
can only learn one projection of the query-key similarity landscape. MHA learns
— information from one head can influence how another head's output is used downstream.
parallel scaled dot-product attention operations, each in a
subspace, then concatenates and projects the results.
scaling factor is essential: it prevents softmax from saturating when
Different heads specialize in different linguistic and structural relationships; this specialization emerges from training, not explicit design.
KV cache is the dominant inference memory cost. Grouped-query attention (GQA) reduces it by sharing K/V projections across query-head groups.
Common bugs include omitting the scale factor, broadcasting masks incorrectly, and confusing self-attention with cross-attention.
For production LLM serving, prefer GQA variants (LLaMA-2, Mistral) over standard MHA to reduce KV cache memory.
Attention weight visualization is a practical diagnostic: entropy collapsing to zero across all heads signals a training pathology.
